text: worked his way deeper inside, clearing some of the rubble from a hallway to access another section . Debris blanketed the floor; rotted bits of plaster, shards of glass, shreds of dingy carpet, and pieces of unidentifiable furniture. A padlocked door stood at the end of the hall. The heavy lock gave way with a single blow, and the entire door came off its hinges when Ronin pulled it open. Inside stood three service bots, one with a broom in hand, their once shining casings dulled with age and dust . The ceiling was reinforced, showing no signs of rot. Ronin had encountered rooms like this one in other places. Were there rules, in the old world, about the sorts of rooms bots had to be stored in ? Stepping inside, he searched the shelves lining the walls, stuffing six long-dead power cells and several specialized tools into his bag. A small box held miscellaneous parts, unidentifiable because of the built-up dust and lint. He placed the entire thing in his bag, atop the other items, but he left the bots alone . Once, they’d moved and worked and spoken and thought. Even if their functions were simple, they’d been alive . Returning to the hallway, Ronin crouched and rummaged through his bag, checking his haul. The tools and assorted parts would undoubtedly bring in a stack of credit, but the power cells were the real treasure. Even uncharged, they alone were likely to bring in as much as his entire last haul . When Ronin emerged from the building, the sun was at its zenith, shrouded by the gray of the sky. The haze was often mistaken for clouds, but Ronin knew if for what it was. The Dust wasn’t just beneath his boots; it was everywhere, over and under, outside and inside. Even with his skin fully replaced, it was only a matter of time before it built up in his internal mechanisms, before it caused internal failures and his eventual breakdown. No one — whether bot or human — escaped the Dust . Not even within the walls of Cheyenne . Lara was still alone. Was she frightened? Upset ? Lonely ? Perhaps she was brimming with anger, because Ronin hadn’t said goodbye; he had departed while she slept . He didn’t want to dwell on regrets. No, if he was never to see Lara again — and he couldn’t dismiss that possibility, even as he railed against it — he wanted to remember her saying she would wait for him. The look in her eyes had spoken the truth. She would actually miss him . His plan had been to scavenge until just before dawn on the third day, to maximize his time here. There was more to find, he knew, and hundreds of thousands of square feet to cover in these buildings . But Lara was alone . Traveling during midday — especially when entering or leaving known scavenging spots like this — was dangerous. It left him exposed. In the open Dust, reliable hiding places were difficult to come by. The decaying buildings of Fort Collins, however, provided ample cover for would-be ambushers. There was no guarantee that he hadn’t been spotted by the hill- dwellers . The safest course would be to shelter in one of the buildings, wait out nightfall, and leave under cover of darkness . Sensors pulsed on his cheek, and he scratched at it absently. Would six or seven more hours make a difference? Undoubtedly. But which effect was more important — the one on his haul, or the one on the woman waiting for him in Cheyenne ? For all the years he’d been awake, this was a new experience, an unexplored dilemma. He’d never had anyone waiting for him. Not a bot, not a human, not anything. There’d been no companionship. His interactions had been limited to passing conversation, negotiations over trades, the occasional night with a female, and the even rarer peaceful encounter in the wastes . He cinched the top of his bag, closed the flap, and slung it over his shoulders. As he stood, he slipped his right arm through the strap of his rifle. His hand settled on the familiar grip, thumb sliding to the indentation worn over decades of use. Glancing down, he performed an inventory of his hand tools, ensuring they were all in place . The trees would provide some cover while he made his way out of town. Beyond that, he’d be back in the Dust, visible from miles away. At least he’d have the same field of view . * * * T hough the sun was still in the sky, the lights of Cheyenne were on as Ronin approached from the eastern railway, which connected to the path between the Bot District and human slums. He’d followed the north road out of Fort Collins. Varying his route helped avoid potential ambushes on the return trip . The railyard had only been partially cleared in all its years of disuse. Hulking train cars, metal and wood having been stripped from many of their frames, lay silent amidst the dunes that had gathered around them. A few were in jumbled heaps of twisted, scorched metal, covered in rust and grime . Movement caught his eye ahead, just off the path. He enhanced his optics, zooming in on the source. A murder of crows, picking at a carcass; their ragged caws drifted to him on the westbound wind just as he obtained a visual . The birds’ meal became more apparent as Ronin neared. His steps slowed. The crows continued their calls, jerking their heads aside to stare at him with black eyes and bits of flesh dangling from their beaks . He surged forward, startling the creatures into scattering. They regrouped on a nearby train car to watch him . Time lost meaning as he stared at what the birds had been feeding upon. Five minutes and seven seconds
